name: Laotian Khao Piak Sen

ingredients.note: thick, or tapioca noodles if available

steps: Simmer the chicken with the onion in the broth until tender, about 20 minutes, then shred it.
Cook the noodles separately according to package directions.
Combine the noodles, shredded chicken, and broth in a bowl.

ingredients_full.note: thick, or tapioca noodles if available

steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken with the onion and garlic in the broth for 20-25 minutes until very tender, then remove and shred it, returning it to the pot.
Stir in the fish sauce.
Cook the noodles according to package directions and add to the broth, or ladle the broth over them.
Top with fresh cilantro - this thick, chicken-based noodle soup is Laos's beloved comfort food, often eaten for breakfast.
description: Khao piak sen, a comforting chicken and tapioca noodle soup, considered close to Laos's national dish.

name: Laotian Sticky Rice with Egg
steps: Cook the rice.
Scramble the eggs with the fish sauce and serve alongside.

ingredients_full.note: soaked at least 4 hours or overnight

steps_detailed: Drain the soaked rice and steam over simmering water in a cloth-lined steamer basket, 20-25 minutes, until translucent and tender - sticky rice is steamed, never boiled directly in water, or it turns to mush.
Scramble the eggs in the oil with the fish sauce.
Serve the steamed sticky rice alongside, eaten by hand, pinched into small balls and dipped into the egg.
description: Sticky rice, Laos's true staple grain, paired here simply with egg.

name: Laotian Coconut Rice Porridge
steps: Simmer the rice in the coconut milk and water for 25-30 minutes until thick.
steps_detailed: Simmer the glutinous rice in the coconut milk and 0.75 cup water over low heat, stirring often, 30-35 minutes, until it breaks down into a thick, sticky porridge - glutinous rice thickens far more than jasmine or basmati would, giving Lao rice porridge its characteristic dense, clingy texture.
Stir in the honey.
description: A simple coconut rice porridge, built on real glutinous rice for the proper dense, sticky texture.

name: Laotian Larb
steps: Brown the beef, then toss with the lime juice, fish sauce, and cilantro.

ingredients_full.note: toasted and ground, standing in for khao khua toasted rice powder

steps_detailed: Toast the raw glutinous rice grains in a dry pan over medium heat, stirring constantly, 4-5 minutes, until deeply golden brown and fragrant, then grind to a coarse powder - this toasted rice powder, khao khua, is what gives larb its distinctive nutty crunch and is not optional or decorative.
Brown the beef in a dry pan, breaking it up, 6-8 minutes, until just cooked through.
Off the heat, toss the hot beef with the lime juice, fish sauce, and chili powder.
Fold in the onion, cilantro, and mint, then finish with the toasted rice powder.

Larb, considered something close to a national dish of Laos, is built on the tension between hot meat and cold raw herbs added at the very last moment - the herbs should still be crisp and bright, not wilted from the meat's heat.
description: Larb, considered something close to Laos's national dish, is built on the tension between hot meat and cold raw herbs added at the very last moment.

name: Laotian Tam Mak Hoong

ingredients.note: shredded, as a green papaya stand-in

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Toss the shredded cabbage with the tomatoes and lime juice.
Top with the crushed peanuts.
Season with salt and serve.

ingredients_full.note: shredded, as a green papaya stand-in

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Toss the shredded cabbage (a stand-in for shredded green papaya, the traditional base) with the tomatoes, garlic, lime juice, and fish sauce.
Top with the crushed peanuts.

The real dish is traditionally pounded together in a mortar and pestle to bruise and soften the vegetables, releasing their juices - Laos's version of the papaya salad found across the region, typically the spiciest.
description: Tam mak hoong, a pounded green papaya salad, the Lao cousin of Thai som tam.

name: Laotian Or Lam
steps: Simmer the pork in the broth 25-30 minutes until nearly tender.
Add the green beans and eggplant, and simmer 12-15 more minutes until all the vegetables are tender.
Serve with sticky rice.

ingredients_full.note: tsp, as a stand-in for local herbs

steps_detailed: Simmer the pork with the garlic in the chicken broth for 25-30 minutes until nearly tender.
Add the green beans, eggplant, fish sauce, and oregano, and simmer 12-18 more minutes until all the vegetables are tender and the stew has thickened slightly.
Serve with sticky rice - a hearty, herb-forward stew from Luang Prabang, considered one of Laos's most distinctive regional dishes.
description: Or lam, a stew traditionally built on sakhan (a bitter, numbing local wood or bark unique to Laos, not a distinct item here) and various forest vegetables and herbs - dried oregano stands in for a piece of that herbal complexity, though the real ingredient's specific numbing quality isn't replicated.

name: Laotian Naem Khao

ingredients.note: standing in for fermented pork sausage

steps: Brown the pork.
Toss with the rice, lime juice, and fish sauce.

ingredients_full.note: standing in for fermented pork sausage (naem)

steps_detailed: Brown the pork, breaking it up, 6-8 minutes, until cooked through.
Toss the cooked, cooled sticky rice with the pork, lime juice, and fish sauce, breaking up any rice clumps so the dressing coats every grain.
Fold in the cilantro, onion, and pepper.

Naem khao is properly built on naem, a specific fermented raw pork sausage that isn't a distinct product in this database - cooked ground pork carries the dish's texture and heartiness without the fermented tang, an honest but real difference from the traditional version.
description: Naem khao, crispy rice tossed with fermented pork sausage, lime, and herbs, a beloved Lao dish.

name: Laotian Grilled Chicken (Ping Kai)
steps: Combine the garlic, fish sauce, and lime juice into a marinade, and coat the chicken.
Marinate at least 20 minutes, then grill or roast until well charred and cooked through, 25-30 minutes.
Serve with sticky rice.
steps_detailed: Combine the garlic, ginger, fish sauce, lime juice, and black pepper into a marinade.
Coat the chicken thoroughly and marinate at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor.
Grill over medium-high heat, or roast at 425F (220C), turning occasionally, 28-32 minutes until well charred and cooked through.
Serve with sticky rice and a dipping sauce - grilled chicken is a fixture of Laotian street food and gatherings.
description: Ping kai, grilled marinated chicken, a Lao street food and festival staple.

name: Laotian Mok Pa (Steamed Fish)
steps: Combine the fish with the coconut milk, fish sauce, and onion.
Wrap in foil (traditionally banana leaf) and steam or bake at 375F for 20-25 minutes until cooked through.
Serve with sticky rice.
steps_detailed: Combine the fish with the coconut milk, fish sauce, onion, garlic, and cilantro in a bowl, coating well.
Wrap tightly in foil (traditionally banana leaf, which adds its own aroma) and steam over simmering water, or bake at 375F (190C), for 22-28 minutes until the fish is cooked through and flaky.
Serve with sticky rice - mok pa is a beloved steamed fish custard, gently spiced and delicate.
description: Mok pa, fish steamed in banana leaf with herbs, a genuine Lao specialty.

name: Laotian Sausage (Sai Oua)
steps: Mix the ground pork with the garlic, ginger, and fish sauce.
Shape into small patties or logs and grill or pan-fry until well browned and cooked through, 5-6 minutes per side.
Serve with sticky rice.
steps_detailed: Mix the ground pork thoroughly with the garlic, ginger, fish sauce, cilantro, and black pepper until well combined.
Shape into small patties or short logs.
Grill or pan-fry over medium heat, 5-7 minutes per side, until well browned and cooked through.
Serve with sticky rice and fresh herbs - sai oua is one of Laos's most celebrated grilled sausages, especially associated with Luang Prabang.
description: Sai oua, herb-packed grilled sausage, a Lao specialty especially associated with the north.

name: Laotian Beef Stew (Aw Sii In)

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the broth and fish sauce, and simmer, covered, 1.5-2 hours until the beef is tender.
Serve with sticky rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

ingredients_full.note: tsp, as a stand-in for local herbs

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion and garlic over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the beef broth, fish sauce, and oregano.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.5-2 hours until the beef is completely tender and the broth has deepened in flavor.
Serve with sticky rice - a warming, herb-forward Laotian beef stew.
description: A hearty Lao beef stew.

name: Laotian Fried Spring Rolls
steps: Brown the chicken and mix with the cabbage.
Roll tightly in the wrapper, sealing the edge with a little water.
Fry until golden and crisp, about 5-6 minutes.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground chicken with the onion, then mix with the shredded cabbage and fish sauce, cooking briefly until the cabbage just softens.
Let cool slightly, then spoon onto the wrapper and roll tightly, sealing the edge with a flour-water paste.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, turning occasionally, 5-7 minutes until deeply golden and crisp on all sides.
A popular snack shared across Laos and its neighbors.
description: Fried spring rolls, shared across the wider Southeast Asian region with Lao seasoning.

name: Laotian Grilled Sticky Rice Balls (Khao Jee)
steps: Form the rice into a ball, brush with the egg and fish sauce, and grill or broil, turning, 8-10 minutes.
steps_detailed: Form the cooked sticky rice into a flattened ball around a skewer, if available.
Brush with the beaten egg mixed with fish sauce and a little oil.
Grill or toast under a broiler, turning and re-brushing every couple of minutes, 8-10 minutes total, until the outside is crisp and deeply golden.
Khao jee, grilled sticky rice basted repeatedly with egg, is a common roadside snack across Laos - the repeated basting and turning is what builds the crisp, lacquered crust.
description: Khao jee, grilled sticky rice basted repeatedly with egg, is a common roadside snack across Laos.

name: Laotian Banana in Coconut Sauce
steps: Warm the coconut milk with the sugar until dissolved.
Add the banana slices and simmer 5-6 minutes until softened.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Warm the coconut milk with the sugar over medium-low heat until the sugar dissolves, 2-3 minutes.
Add the banana slices and simmer gently 6-8 minutes until softened and infused with the coconut sauce.
Top with the toasted shredded coconut and serve warm - a simple, comforting sweet snack found throughout Laos.
description: A simple banana and coconut milk dessert.

name: Laotian Spiced Fried Peanuts

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Fry the sliced garlic in the oil until golden.
Add the peanuts and toss to coat, warming through 1-2 minutes.
Cool slightly before serving.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Fry the sliced garlic in the oil over medium-low heat, 2-3 minutes, until golden and crisp - watch closely, as it burns quickly.
Add the peanuts and turmeric, and toss to coat, warming through for 1-2 minutes.
Cool slightly before serving - a simple, savory snack, often set out alongside drinks.
description: A simple spiced peanut snack.

name: Laotian Sticky Rice with Mango

ingredients.ingredient: Apples, raw, granny smith, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Cook the rice, then stir through the sweetened coconut milk.
Serve with the fruit.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Apples, raw, granny smith, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Drain the soaked rice and steam in a cloth-lined steamer basket, 20-25 minutes, until translucent and tender.
Warm the coconut milk with the sugar until dissolved, then pour most of it over the warm rice and let it absorb 10 minutes.
Serve the sweetened sticky rice with the fruit alongside, drizzling the reserved coconut milk over both.

Ripe mango isn't a distinct raw item in this database, so a firmer fruit stands in here - the real dish depends on mango's specific soft, perfumed ripeness, which this substitute doesn't fully replicate.
description: Sweet sticky rice with fruit, built on real glutinous rice.

name: Laotian Coconut Custard
steps: Whisk the eggs, coconut milk, and sugar together until smooth.
Pour into a small dish and steam or bake at 325F for 30-35 minutes until just set.
Cool before serving.
steps_detailed: Whisk the eggs, coconut milk, sugar, and vanilla together until smooth and well combined.
Pour into a small buttered dish.
Steam over simmering water, or bake in a water bath at 325F (165C), for 32-40 minutes until just set with a gentle wobble in the center.
Cool before serving, chilled or at room temperature - a simple, silky coconut custard found across Laos.
description: A simple coconut custard.

name: Laotian Khao Tom (Sweet Rice Bundles)
steps: Mix the rice with the coconut milk, sugar, and plantain.
Steam in a foil packet 30-35 minutes.
steps_detailed: Drain the soaked rice and toss with the coconut milk and sugar.

Traditionally wrapped and steamed in banana leaves, which aren't a distinct item in this database - instead, spread the rice mixture with the plantain in a small foil packet, fold to seal, and steam over simmering water 30-35 minutes until the rice is fully tender.

Khao tom, sweet sticky rice bundles with banana or other fruit steamed inside, are made for Lao festivals and merit-making ceremonies - the banana-leaf wrapping is traditional for both flavor and presentation; foil keeps the same steaming method without it.
description: Khao tom, sweet sticky rice bundles with fruit steamed inside, made for Lao festivals and merit-making ceremonies.

name: Laotian Jeow Mak Len (Tomato Dip)
steps: Simmer the tomatoes with the garlic for 8-10 minutes until thickened.
Stir in the fish sauce and lime juice.
Serve with sticky rice and grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Simmer the tomatoes with the garlic and jalapeno (if using) over medium-low heat for 10-12 minutes until thickened and jammy.
Mash roughly with a fork or in a mortar for a more traditional texture, then stir in the fish sauce and lime juice.
Serve as a dip with sticky rice and grilled meats or vegetables - jeow dips are a staple of Laotian meals.
description: Jeow, a category of Lao dipping sauces, this version built on charred tomato.

name: Laotian Padaek-Style Chili Sauce
steps: Whisk the fish sauce, jalapeno, and lime juice together.
Serve as a dipping sauce alongside rice and grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Whisk the fish sauce, jalapeno, lime juice, and garlic together until combined.
Serve as a bold, pungent dipping sauce - padaek, a fermented fish sauce even more intense than standard fish sauce, is central to Laotian cooking; this offers a similar savory heat in a simpler form.
description: Real Lao chili sauces often lean on padaek, a strong fermented fish sauce distinct from the lighter Thai-style fish sauce - a genuinely different, funkier product not available here, so this version stays milder than the real thing.
